segments.text:
MICROFILAMENTS
Subunit
actin protein
G-actin (globular)
binds ATP or ADP
Polymer
F-actin (filamentous)
Polar organization
(+) and (-) ends

ACTIN POLYMERIZATION
Nucleation (trimer formation) = rate limiting step

ACTIN FILAMENT GROWTH
(+) end grows faster than (-) end
ATP hydrolyzed and Pi released within filament
(+) end
(-) end

MICROFILAMENT TOXINS
Cytochalasin
fungal toxin
binds (+) end of filaments
prevents polymerization
Phalloidin
fungal toxin (Angel of Death mushroom)
binds sides of filaments
stabilizes filaments

Focal adhesions – attach cytoskeleton to ECM through integrins
Stress fibers – contain actin/myosin contractile bundles
FOCAL ADHESIONS AND STRESS FIBERS

LEADING EDGE
Lamellipodium – flat extended structure
Filipodium – finger-like extension
Filipodia

REGULATION OF ACTIN STRUCTURES
Controlled by small guanine nucleotide binding “Rho proteins”
Rho
Rac
Cdc42

REGULATION OF ACTIN STRUCTURES
Rac: lamellipodia formation
Cdc42: filopodia formation
Rho: stress fiber formation and contraction
external signal

ARP 2/3 COMPLEX
Nucleating complex
Arp = actin-related protein
Arp2 and Arp 3 mimic nucleus
Requires activator (WASp or WAVE)

ARP 2/3 COMPLEX
Binds to side of pre-existing filament
Forms branching pattern
Remains at (-) end

ARP 2/3 COMPLEX REGULATION
Intramolecular inhibition of WASp
Activated Cdc42 (GTP bound) binds RBD
Arp2/3 activating domain (purple) binds Arp2/3 (blue)
Similar for Rac activation of WAVE

Capping protein
CapZ
caps (+) end
prevents growth at (+) end

cofilin
Binds along actin-ADP part of filament
Depolymerizes filament by breaking off (-) ends

MONOMER-BINDING PROTEIN
Profilin: increases nucleotide exchange (more ATP-actin available)
